query: current guidelines for treatment of anaphylactic shock

retrieved_docs.title: Anaphylaxis -- Treatment / Management -- Vasopressors
retrieved_docs.content: Vasopressors may be substituted when a patient requires more doses of epinephrine but have unacceptable side effects from the epinephrine intravenous infusion (arrhythmia or chest pain). If no clear second-line pressor is identified, treatment guidelines would follow those of any other patient in hypotensive shock.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaphylaxis -- Evaluation -- Clinical Criteria for Anaphylaxis (1 of the following with onset within minutes to hours)*
retrieved_docs.content: Known exposure to an antigen and hypotension (systolic less than 90 mm Hg or a decrease of greater than 30% from baseline). It is not required to meet these criteria to treat; it is only to serve as a guide for diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Allergy Immunotherapy -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: The use of concomitant B-blockers is contraindicated with immunotherapy in the rare chance that the patient needs epinephrine to treat anaphylaxis. However, according to the U.S. practice parameters on anaphylaxis, the benefits of Hymenoptera venom immunotherapy (VIT) outweigh the potential risks associated with b-blockers or ACEIs in patients with anaphylaxis to stinging insects who also have cardiovascular disease that requires these medications. [14] As per the EAACI guidelines, the risk/benefit profile also suggests that there should be no contraindication for venom immunotherapy in patients on B-blockers. [15] If a patient is having an asthma exacerbation, they should not receive an immunotherapy injection [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anaphylaxis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- An Evidence-based Approach to Anaphylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: Anaphylaxis is a rapid systemic and unpredictable disorder that is life-threatening. The disorder often occurs after exposure to certain antigens, mostly insect venom, food, or medications. The condition is immune-mediated and rapidly induces symptoms. For those who have not encountered anaphylaxis, the diagnosis can be difficult. In the United States, the rates of anaphylaxis have doubled over the past 20 years, and at least 1500 people die from the disorder each year. The potentially life-threatening nature of the disorder makes it imperative that one be prepared and offer prompt treatment. This is 1 of the few medical disorders during which a healthcare worker does not have the luxury of time. Having epinephrine on hand can be life-saving if one wants to avoid a fatal outcome. However, despite considerable evidence that epinephrine is the drug of choice, it is under-used. Instead, many healthcare workers use an antihistamine, a drug that does not prevent or relieve all the manifestations of anaphylaxis. [20] In addition, the antihistamine does not even start to work for 1 to 3 hours, depending on the route of administration. Given these findings, all healthcare workers must be educated on anaphylaxis and its treatment. Evidence indicates that healthcare workers are critical for managing and preventing anaphylaxis.

retrieved_docs.title: Epinephrine -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Epinephrine is currently approved by the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for several indications, including the emergency treatment of type 1 hypersensitivity reactions such as anaphylaxis, the induction and maintenance of mydriasis during intraocular surgeries, and the management of hypotension due to septic shock. [2] [3] [4] [5] The American College of Allergy, Asthma, and Immunology (ACAAI) advises clinicians to counsel high-risk patients to carry a self-injectable epinephrine device at all times and ensure they are properly educated on its indications and correct use. Clinicians are advised to regularly prescribe epinephrine auto-injectors (EAIs) for patients at increased risk of anaphylaxis.

retrieved_docs.title: Diphenhydramine -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoids and antihistamines may also be considered to prevent anaphylaxis or infusion-related reactions when indicated for specific chemotherapy agents. [2] According to the National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN) guidelines, anaphylactic symptoms in mastocytosis should be treated with epinephrine as first-line therapy. Antihistamines (H1 and H2 blockers) and steroids can be added as needed. Systemic hives without organ involvement can be managed with antihistamines. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anaphylaxis -- Treatment / Management -- Triage
retrieved_docs.content: Triage any allergic reaction with urgency as they are at risk for rapid deterioration with the development of anaphylaxis, if not already anaphylactic. [15] [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Anaphylaxis -- Treatment / Management -- IV Fluid Resuscitation
retrieved_docs.content: Anaphylaxis induces a distributive shock that typically is responsive to fluid resuscitation and the above epinephrine. One to 2 L or 10 to 20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid bolus should be given for observed hypotension. Albumin or hypertonic solutions are not indicated.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaphylaxis -- Treatment / Management -- Adjunctive Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: When anaphylaxis is diagnosed, co-treatment is often initiated with steroids, antihistamines, inhaled bronchodilators, and vasopressors. Glucagon can also be used if indicated. These agents can assist in refractory initial anaphylaxis or prevent recurrence and biphasic reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management -- Epinephrine Dosing
retrieved_docs.content: The recommended dose of epinephrine is 0.01 mg/kg, up to a maximum of 0.5 mg, which may be repeated every 5 to 15 minutes as necessary. About 35% of patients require a second dose. In the United States, epinephrine ampules are now labeled as 1 mg/mL (formerly 1:1000). Autoinjectors are available in 0.1, 0.15, and 0.3 mg doses. When using autoinjectors or when there may be delays in weight-based dosing, the guidelines should be followed: Less than 10 kg: Use a 0.1 mg autoinjector or draw 0.1 mL of 1 mg/mL solution; 0.15 mg is acceptable if 0.1 mg is unavailable 10 to 25 kg: Use a 0.15 mg autoinjector or draw 0.15 mL 25 to 50 kg: Use a 0.3 mg autoinjector or draw 0.3 mL More than 50 kg: Draw 0.5 mL; if unavailable, use a 0.3 mg autoinjector [39] [40] [41] Experts define refractory anaphylaxis as persistent symptoms after 3 epinephrine doses and supportive care. These patients may require an IV epinephrine infusion:

retrieved_docs.title: Evinacumab -- Contraindications -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Evinacumab is contraindicated in patients with severe hypersensitivity reactions such as anaphylaxis. [13] Patients with a history of allergic reactions to any excipients in evinacumab should not be prescribed the medication.

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management -- Anaphylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: High-flow oxygen should be administered using a nonrebreather mask at 15 L/min, and IV access should be established promptly. For stable patients, IV fluids should be started at 125 mL/h of normal saline for adults and at weight-based maintenance rates for children. Intubation should be anticipated in the presence of any respiratory distress, stridor, voice changes, or visible airway swelling. For patients who remain hypotensive after epinephrine, clinicians should begin aggressive fluid resuscitation. Adults typically require 1 to 2 L of normal saline rapidly, whereas children require 20 mL/kg boluses over 5 to 10 min, repeated as needed. Lactated Ringer may lead to metabolic alkalosis; however, large volumes of normal saline can cause hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis, so some experts choose to switch to lactated Ringer when large volumes of normal saline are necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: Omalizumab -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Contraindications include a severe hypersensitivity reaction to omalizumab, and there have been reported cases of anaphylaxis. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management -- Additional Agents
retrieved_docs.content: Combining an H1 antihistamine with an H2 antihistamine, such as famotidine, may help patients achieve a better reduction in urticaria symptoms. However, antihistamines should not be used as monotherapy, as they do not mitigate upper or lower airway obstruction, shock, or hypotension. Antihistamines have not been shown to affect hospitalization rates or epinephrine requirements. Some studies suggest that when H2 antagonists are given IV, they may increase rates of hypotension. The recommended dose of famotidine is 20 mg for adults and 0.25 mg/kg for children, up to a maximum dose of 20 mg.

retrieved_docs.title: Type I Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Effective management of type I hypersensitivity requires coordinated, patient-centered care supported by strong interprofessional collaboration. Clinicians and advanced practitioners play a key role in diagnosis, risk stratification, and treatment planning. Their responsibilities include obtaining a detailed allergy history, ordering appropriate diagnostic tests, and initiating evidence-based interventions such as epinephrine for anaphylaxis or allergen immunotherapy when indicated.

retrieved_docs.title: Anaphylaxis -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- An Evidence-based Approach to Anaphylaxis
retrieved_docs.content: Since people may develop anaphylaxis almost anywhere, all healthcare workers must know the signs and symptoms of the disorder and know how to administer epinephrine. The pharmacist is in a key position to educate the patient and family about anaphylaxis. Plus, the pharmacist can teach the patient and family to carry epinephrine with them all the time. In addition, all physicians, nurses, and other allied healthcare workers need to know the signs and symptoms of anaphylaxis and how to administer epinephrine. The treating physician should reduce all the trigger factors and ensure optimal asthma management. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 306
retrieved_docs.content: Dexamethasone acetate, a corticosteroid formulation, may be employed in the treatment of septic shock (صدمة إنتانيّة). By providing anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects, dexamethasone acetate can help modulate the excessive systemic inflammatory response that characterizes septic shock, potentially improving hemodynamic stability and patient outcomes when used according to appropriate clinical guidelines.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2530
retrieved_docs.content: Tolnaftate is contraindicated in patients who have the hypersensitivity condition فرط تحسس, meaning it should not be used in individuals with this allergic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 340
retrieved_docs.content: emphasizing the need for clinicians to recognize and prevent hypersensitivity‑related adverse events across the entire spectrum of listed pharmaceuticals. Overall, the integrated framework unifies diverse drug classes through a structural‑analogue taxonomy, connects them to a diagnostic radiopharmaceutical, and provides explicit hypersensitivity contraindications, thereby supporting consistent clinical documentation, safe prescribing and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 0 -- Community 8
retrieved_docs.content: clinicians with a structured framework for selecting appropriate bronchodilators, systemic adrenergics, mineralocorticoids and diagnostic tools based on receptor selectivity, route of administration, therapeutic group, contraindications and combination formulations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 0 -- Community 13
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the community’s knowledge delineates how anticholinergic and adrenergic agents are organized by selectivity, route of administration, and combination therapy, and it clarifies their specific indications and contraindications in acute inflammatory respiratory conditions such as acute bronchitis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2779
retrieved_docs.content: Atrakurium, imatinib mesylát, and etodolac are each contraindicated in the presence of فرط الحساسية الدوائية (drug hypersensitivity). This shared contraindication underscores the necessity of evaluating a patient’s allergy history before prescribing any of these medications, as exposure in individuals with drug hypersensitivity could precipitate severe allergic reactions. Proper assessment and avoidance of these agents in susceptible patients are essential for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 659
retrieved_docs.content: Methyldopa, centrally acting antiadrenergic agents, is classified as a type of antiadrenergic agents, centrally acting and is a member of the methyldopa drug group. Methyldopa, the non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug fenoprofen, and the antifungal agents butenafine and tolnaftát are all contraindicated in patients who have hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس). This shared contraindication highlights the importance of assessing allergic risk before prescribing any of these medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2484
retrieved_docs.content: Fenoprofen and butenafine are both contraindicated in the presence of hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس). Use of fenoprofen is not recommended for patients who have a hypersensitivity reaction, and similarly, butenafine should be avoided in individuals with hypersensitivity disease. Recognizing hypersensitivity as a contraindicating factor for these medications is essential for safe prescribing and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 984
retrieved_docs.content: Methyldopa, centrally acting adntiadrenergic agents is classified as an antiadrenergic agents, centrally acting (using an “isa” relationship) and is a member of the broader methyldopa category. Both methyldopa and the non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug fenoprofen have a contraindication for patients with فرط تحسس (hypersensitivity), indicating that these agents should not be used in individuals who are hypersensitive. Similarly, the antifungal medication butenafine is contraindicated with the disease فرط تحسس, reinforcing the need to assess hypersensitivity status before prescribing these drugs. Recognizing and avoiding hypersensitivity reactions to methyldopa, fenoprofen, and butenafine is essential for safe patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: swelling and bronchoconstriction, highlighting its potential therapeutic role in histamine‑mediated conditions. This taxonomy clarifies systemic versus inhaled routes, adrenergic subtypes, and the composition of combination bronchodilator products, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate pharmacotherapy for optimal management of obstructive airway diseases and related allergic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1856
retrieved_docs.content: Fenoprofen, butenafine, and tolnaftát are each contraindicated in patients who have hypersensitivity (فرط تحسس), indicating that these medications should not be administered to individuals with a known allergic or hypersensitivity reaction to avoid adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1856
retrieved_docs.content: Diphenhydramine hydrochloride is contraindicated in patients with حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة (an edematous condition). In contrast, hydrocortisone sodium succinate may be used to treat حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة and can also be employed in the management of الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse). Recognizing that diphenhydramine hydrochloride should be avoided in edema while hydrocortisone sodium succinate is appropriate for both edema and severe circulatory failure guides therapeutic decisions and enhances patient safety.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1938
retrieved_docs.content: Cortisol succinate is identified as a potential therapeutic agent for the management of shock. Shock, a life‑threatening circulatory failure, is classified in medical terminology as “صدمة إنتانيّة” (infectious shock) and also as “صَدْمَة قَلْبِيَّة المَنْشَأ” (cardiogenic shock), reflecting its distinct etiologic subtypes. Recognizing that cortisol succinate may be used to treat these severe shock states underscores its relevance in acute care settings where rapid reversal of hemodynamic collapse is essential.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1360
retrieved_docs.content: Imatinib mesylate, Etodolac, and Sodium selenate are each contraindicated in patients who have فرط الحساسية الدوائية (drug hypersensitivity). Because a known hypersensitivity reaction to medications can lead to severe adverse effects, these drugs should be avoided in individuals with such a history. Recognizing this contraindication is essential for clinicians when evaluating a patient’s allergy profile before initiating therapy with any of these agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1344
retrieved_docs.content: Timolol, a non‑selective beta‑adrenergic blocker, should not be used in patients who have تَشَنُّج قَصَبِيّ (bronchial spasm or asthma) because the drug can worsen airway constriction and precipitate respiratory distress. Recognizing this contraindication is essential for safe prescribing and for preventing adverse respiratory events in individuals with underlying bronchospastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1916
retrieved_docs.content: Diphenhydramine hydrochloride, an antihistamine commonly used for allergic reactions, is contraindicated in حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة (pregnancy), indicating that it should be avoided in pregnant patients because its use may pose risks to the developing fetus.
